Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I would try to set aside time on weekends to catch up, but with everything going on, it rarely worked out as planned. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to recorded lectures while doing other things around the house. Some weeks I'd manage maybe 15 hours, but it wasn't structured at all. I just kind of fit studying in wherever I could, which left me feeling constantly behind.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly a deadline would loom and I'd cram everything in. It wasn't healthy, and I knew it, but the motivation just wasn't there to be consistent. When life got stressful, studying was the first thing to slip. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but that would be a lie.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something due soon, and that would jolt me into action. Occasionally I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which happened more often than I'd like to admit. Fear of failing was honestly my biggest motivator, which isn't a great feeling to carry around.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were brief periods where I tried to make it a habit, like checking in every morning, but it never stuck. The interface felt overwhelming sometimes, and I'd log in, feel anxious about how much I hadn't done, and log out again. It never became natural for me the way I think it does for some students.
Q5: The audio recordings and any video content were most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I could play lectures while commuting or doing chores, which helped me absorb information without having to sit and stare at a screen. The discussion posts were sometimes helpful too, because hearing other students' perspectives in my head as I read them helped concepts click. Anything I could listen to repeatedly was gold for me.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and PDFs. They just sat there and I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The discussion forums I participated in sometimes, but not as much as I should have. I think I avoided things that required sustained visual attention because it felt exhausting, and my motivation was already so low.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to the exam or assignments. If something seemed like busy work or optional enrichment, I'd skip it because I was already struggling to keep up with the essentials. Anything that I could engage with through listening rather than reading also felt more worthwhile to me. The moment something felt abstract or disconnected from my grade, I'd lose interest.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're scared about keeping up with the basics, optional stuff just adds to the guilt pile. Required activities had urgency attached to them, so I'd force myself through them even when I didn't want to. Optional ones would sit in the back of my mind, making me feel bad for not doing them, but never bad enough to actually complete them.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio files and listened to them on my phone offline. I also made voice memos of myself summarizing concepts, which I'd replay while walking or before bed. My notes were handwritten because I remember things better when I hear myself say them out loud as I write. None of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I was studying intensely but the LMS would show nothing. I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times, talk through problems out loud, or have study sessions with a friend where we'd quiz each other verbally. I also spent a lot of time just thinking through concepts while doing mundane tasks. My exam scores reflect that learning happened, but my LMS activity definitely doesn't capture it.
Q11: All the time. I'd download audio lectures once and then listen to them repeatedly over several weeks without ever logging back in. Same with any PDFs I did manage to save—I'd access them locally rather than through the platform. It was just easier and didn't require internet, plus I didn't have to face the anxiety of seeing all my incomplete tasks every time I wanted to study.
Q12: There were definitely moments when I felt paralyzed. Around midterms, everything piled up and I felt this overwhelming sense of dread. I'd log in, see all the things I hadn't done, and feel so scared that I'd just close the browser. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but it was hard to break. When stress peaked, engagement dropped to almost nothing.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or had family responsibilities competing for my attention, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion about what was actually required versus optional also made me hesitant to engage—I'd waste time trying to figure out priorities instead of actually studying. Fatigue made everything feel impossible, and the medium resources I have meant I couldn't always access help when I needed it.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have the LMS open but I was just scrolling mindlessly, not really absorbing anything. I'd watch videos but realize at the end that I hadn't retained a single thing because my mind was elsewhere. Those sessions felt like going through the motions to ease my guilt rather than actually learning. It was frustrating because I'd spent the time but had nothing to show for it.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The fear of not doing them well enough made it easier to just... not start. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace, but that headspace never came. By the time I finally did them, I was rushed and stressed, which probably hurt my assignment scores. It's a cycle I'm still trying to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage with the LMS seriously. I'd have bursts of activity right before something was due, then go quiet again. It wasn't sustainable and it was stressful, but without that external pressure, I couldn't seem to make myself do anything. My whole engagement pattern was shaped by fear of missing deadlines rather than genuine interest.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always positively. Good feedback gave me a brief boost, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid the course entirely. After getting feedback on an assignment, I'd either feel motivated to do better or scared to try again. The fear usually won out, which meant I'd delay looking at feedback for as long as possible.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming at times. There was so much content that I never felt like I could get on top of it. The lack of clear prioritization made it hard to know where to focus my limited energy. I think if the course had been more modular or had clearer milestones, I might have engaged more consistently instead of in panicked bursts.
Q19: The availability of audio content made engagement easier for me as an auditory learner. But the sheer volume of materials and the way everything was laid out made it harder—I'd feel lost and anxious just looking at the course page. Having to navigate through multiple sections to find what I needed added friction. Simpler navigation and clearer priorities would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways—logging in, looking around, feeling overwhelmed, and then not actually completing anything. High activity could mean someone is struggling to understand what to do or is anxious and checking repeatedly without making progress. It could also be someone who engages with content but freezes when it comes to actually submitting work.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My attendance was low and my LMS engagement wasn't great, but I still got a Distinction because I found ways to learn that worked for me outside the system. Listening to downloaded content, self-quizzing, talking through concepts—none of that shows up in logs. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing at all.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, how often, what they click on. But they miss the quality of engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture whether someone was actually thinking deeply or just scrolling. They definitely can't show the emotional experience of engaging with a course, the fear, the avoidance, the small victories.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, asking myself questions, trying to connect ideas. It's not about how many times I log in or how long I watch videos. Real engagement is mental and emotional investment, which is hard to measure from the outside.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how much that fear shaped my behavior. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures offline or talking through problems with friends. It wouldn't show the CBT sessions that helped me manage my anxiety around the course. My relationship with this course was complicated, and the data only tells a tiny part of that story.
